data.title: Antero Resources upgraded, Diamondback and others downgraded at Benchmark
data.description:
Antero Resources (AR) +3.6% in Thursday's trading as Benchmark upgraded to Buy from Hold with a $44 price target, saying the shares have significantly trailed other oil and gas producers as gas prices withdrew from multi-year highs on strong supply.

Benchmark's Subash Chandra is not calling the bottom on natural gas but feels increasingly positive on natural gas exporters, which should enjoy a bump from the war in Iran as stable sources of supply, noting the U.S. is the third largest exporter of propane after Qatar and the United Arab Emirates, two countries that are now in a war zone.

NGLs represent a smaller share of Antero's (AR) business pro forma for the acquisition of HG Energy II, but the company is the largest publicly traded NGL exporter, Chandra said, adding Antero's dry gas business should yield a lower breakeven cost without sacrificing basis differentials given the company's pathways to the LNG corridor.

Also, Chandra cut Talos Energy (TALO) to Hold from Buy following FY 2026 guidance was softer than expected despite a strong 2025, adding the 2026 drilling program is "vigorous and meaningful but recurring challenges to base production persist."

The analyst also downgraded Diamondback Energy (FANG) and Permian Resources (PR) to Hold from Buy, citing valuation.

data.title: Rocket Cos., UWM Holdings slide after Better partners with OpenAI on mortgage decision app
data.description:
Rocket Companies (RKT) stock fell 5.1%, and UWM Holdings (UWMC) stock declined 4.2% in Thursday afternoon trading after Better.com partnered with OpenAI (OPENAI) to launch a conversational credit decision engine in ChatGPT.

The decision app for  mortgages and home equity loans connects users with Better's Tinman engine directly in ChatGPT, allowing lending teams to approve more loans faster, the companies said in a statement.

"Big mortgage aggregators in the broker and correspondent channel charge what is essentially a 1-2% tax on each loan just to underwrite a mortgage and deliver it to an institutional investor. That ends now," said Leah Price, general manager of the Tinman AI Platform.

Other lending platforms also slumped. LendingClub (LC) dropped 3.6%, and LendingTree (TREE) fell 1.8%.

data.title: Intrepid projects 7% Trio production growth for 2026 while deferring AMAX decision
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Intrepid Potash, Inc. (IPI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Kevin Crutchfield stated that Intrepid delivered strong fourth quarter results, highlighting adjusted net income of $6.5 million and adjusted EBITDA of $18.1 million, with a full-year adjusted EBITDA of $63 million, "one of the best prints since 2016 and represents an almost 80% improvement compared to 2024."
  • Crutchfield attributed the performance to steady demand for core fertilizer products, with combined potash and Trio sales volumes of just over 590,000 tons, a 20% increase from 2024, and a company record of 303,000 tons of Trio sales.
  • The CEO emphasized improvements in cost of goods sold per ton for both potash and Trio and noted "increasing pricing"—especially in Trio, where fourth quarter average realized price hit $379 per ton, 20% higher than the first quarter of 2025.
  • Strategic updates included deferring a decision on the AMAX cavern project until at least 2027 and confidence in sustaining HB potash production without AMAX. Crutchfield announced, "For Trio, our operational performance continues to be very strong, and we recently placed another new continuous miner into service."
  • The CEO discussed progress on the Wendover lithium project, mentioning a joint development agreement with Aquatech and Adionics and a forthcoming technical report showing a measured and indicated resource of approximately 119,000 tons of lithium carbonate equivalent, with a potential project life of roughly 25 years at a 5,000-ton-per-year estimated capacity.
  • There is an $8 million deposit held from a potential buyer for South Ranch, with expectations that a deal could close in the first half of 2026.
  • CFO Matt Preston reported, "Our total fertilizer sales volumes of 592,000 tons were almost 100,000 tons higher than 2024 and reached a level not seen since 2018."

Outlook

  • Preston provided 2026 production guidance, expecting Trio output of 285,000 to 300,000 tons, representing a year-over-year increase of about 7% at the midpoint, and annual potash production anticipated between 270,000 and 285,000 tons.
  • First quarter 2026 sales guidance is 95,000 to 105,000 tons of potash at a net realized sales price of $345 to $355 per ton, and 105,000 to 115,000 tons of Trio at a price of $380 to $390 per ton.
  • Capital investment for 2026 is projected at $40 million to $50 million, largely directed at sustaining capital projects, including East mine and the new primary pond at Wendover.

Financial Results

  • The company reported that potash fourth quarter gross margin was $4.6 million, with full-year segment gross margin of $18.2 million. Potash sales volumes increased 20% year-over-year, offsetting a $25 per ton decline in pricing.
  • Trio's gross margin was $10.5 million in Q4 and $33.4 million for the year, with 2025 described as the "best Trio performance in our history" outside of the elevated pricing of 2022.
  • Preston noted "production improvements in our Trio segment going from 216,000 tons in 2023 to nearly 300,000 tons in 2026 are sustainable."

Q&A

  • Nicole Greenberg, UBS: Asked about potash demand dynamics and order book for Q1, as well as any evidence of demand disruption. Zachry Adams, Vice President of Sales & Marketing, responded, "We're almost fully committed for first quarter right now on potash, and we have not seen really any significant demand disruption at this time."
  • Greenberg queried the lithium project's unit economics. Crutchfield replied, "We're not prepared to address that at this stage."
  • Greenberg asked about oilfield sales outlook. Crutchfield explained the intent to transact on the oilfield asset, referencing a letter of intent with a prospective buyer.
  • Justin Pellegrino, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about sulfur prices and Trio interest. Rick Kim noted, "We're seeing really good response, I would say, just from a demand perspective for the rest of first quarter out into the second quarter at this point."
  • Pellegrino questioned capital allocation priorities if the South Ranch deal closes. Crutchfield explained, "Our first priority is an intense focus on our core operations...making sure that they're generating consistent free cash flow...then that's when we can enter a period of what does a capital return policy begin to look like."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, with questions focused on demand, project economics, and capital allocation. There were no signs of skepticism or pressing challenges.
  • Management's tone was confident in prepared remarks and maintained composure during Q&A, occasionally deflecting, as seen in Crutchfield's response, "We're not prepared to address that at this stage."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management and analyst sentiment remained steady, with a continued focus on operational execution and growth projects.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management raised Trio production guidance for 2026 and provided more specific potash production and pricing outlooks. The AMAX project decision was deferred further, reflecting increased caution.
  • Strategic focus shifted slightly toward the lithium project and the South Ranch transaction, while continued operational discipline was reinforced.
  • Analysts maintained focus on core fertilizer demand, project updates, and capital allocation, mirroring themes from the previous quarter.
  • Key metrics such as adjusted EBITDA, sales volumes, and Trio gross margin improved sequentially.
  • Management's confidence in core operations and future growth initiatives was consistent, while the tone remained constructive.

Risks and Concerns

  • Challenges mentioned include slightly down potash production in 2026 due to below-average evaporation at HB and delayed start-up impacting production targets.
  • The AMAX cavern project remains on hold until at least 2027, pending further evaluation of mineralogy and geology.
  • Management acknowledged the need to sustain core operations and maintain liquidity, with capital allocation beyond core needs contingent on continued operational reliability.
  • Analyst concerns included demand sustainability, lithium project economics, and oilfield business outlook, but management generally did not provide detailed financial figures for these areas.

Final Takeaway

Intrepid Potash, Inc. delivered strong fourth quarter and full-year 2025 results, driven by higher fertilizer sales volumes, improved production efficiencies, and firm market demand. Trio production is targeted for further growth in 2026, while the company maintains a cautious approach to new capital projects like AMAX and remains optimistic about the long-term potential of its lithium initiative. With continued operational focus, disciplined capital management, and active pursuit of strategic transactions, Intrepid aims to sustain its momentum heading into 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Altimmune outlines 1,800-patient Phase III MASH trial and signals cash runway into 2028 as pemvidutide advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Altimmune (ALT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jerome Durso, Chairman, CEO & President, emphasized Altimmune's exclusive focus on liver disease and outlined the significant unmet need in MASH, highlighting pemvidutide's "balance one-to-one agonism of glucagon and GLP-1 in a single molecule achieved with pemvi makes it potentially well suited for the conditions we're targeting." He reinforced the drug's potential to reduce liver fat and inflammation as well as its favorable tolerability, noting, "the favorable tolerability leading to low discontinuations due to adverse events that we saw in our Phase II MASH trial can be a key differentiator that pemvi may deliver." Durso also referenced the FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation for pemvidutide in MASH and described preparations for a global Phase III trial.

  • Durso reported the addition of new leaders with expertise in liver disease, late-stage clinical development, and commercial strategy, and highlighted a $75 million capital raise in January to support clinical advancement. He stated, "We reported total cash of $274 million at year-end 2025" and described a pro forma cash position of approximately $340 million after recent fundraising.

  • Christophe Arbet-Engels, Chief Medical Officer, noted that the 48-week data from the IMPACT trial established "a clear dose response that supports our plan to focus on the 1.8 milligram dose in the Phase III trial, while also evaluating the 2.4 milligram dose which could provide additional benefits on both weight loss and most importantly, liver efficacy." He described statistically significant improvements in liver fat content and key noninvasive markers.

  • Linda Richardson, Chief Commercial Officer, shared results from market research showing "over 70% reported a very high or high likelihood to prescribe pemvi," with physicians projecting use in 43% of F2 and 51% of F3 patients. She highlighted differentiation in tolerability and lean muscle preservation.

  • Gregory Weaver, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "R&D expense in the fourth quarter 2025 was $18.4 million compared to $19.8 million in the same period of 2024." Weaver noted, "Net loss for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $27.4 million or $0.27 a share compared to a net loss of $23.2 million or $0.33 a share in the fourth quarter of 2024." Weaver projected, "Our current cash position would provide an operating cash runway into 2028 based on our current expectations for the scope and timing of the MASH Phase III plan along with the cost of both the AUD and ALD Phase II trials."

Outlook

  • Management confirmed plans to initiate a pivotal global Phase III MASH trial in 2026, enrolling approximately 1,800 patients with biopsy-confirmed F2/F3 MASH or NIT-assessed F2/F3 MASH. The trial will evaluate pemvidutide at 1.8 mg and 2.4 mg doses, with primary endpoints of MASH resolution and fibrosis improvement at 52 weeks. Durso stated, "We are clear on the overall design elements and the endpoints, and we're now in the process of making the final detailed decisions on the protocol and the full operational plan."

  • Enrollment of the Phase II AUD trial was completed ahead of schedule, with top-line data expected in Q3 2026. Completion of the Phase II ALD trial enrollment is targeted for 2026.

  • Management reiterated that the cash runway is expected to extend into 2028, supporting ongoing and planned trials.

Financial Results

  • R&D expense in Q4 2025 was $18.4 million, with $12.8 million allocated to pemvidutide development and $7.4 million for Phase II trials in AUD and ALD. G&A expenses were $10.5 million, including a $2.6 million one-time charge related to executive transition. Net loss for Q4 2025 was $27.4 million, or $0.27 per share.

  • Total cash at year-end 2025 was $274 million, with a pro forma cash position of approximately $340 million after additional fundraising in January.

  • Weaver indicated that cash use will "trend up this year as we approach the launch of the MASH Phase III trial."

Q&A

  • Jiale Song, Jefferies: Asked about the FDA's new single pivotal framework and potential for cost savings in Phase III. Arbet-Engels responded, "The path for approval for the MASH programs is one single trial for accelerated approval and then all the way to final approval for clinical outcomes. So this doesn't really apply directly for us."

  • Jasmine Fels, Barclays: Inquired about the FDA's flexibility on NITs as registrational endpoints and the inclusion of F4 patients. Durso explained, "The agency at that point said it was premature to consider that, which is why you see the biopsy driven end points." Arbet-Engels stated, "Our current focus is clearly on the F2/F3."

  • Dominic Lorenzi, Piper Sandler: Questioned timelines for Phase III enrollment. Durso replied, "We expect that all will be in line to start the trial as we progress through this year, and we'll narrow the guidance as things come to fruition."

  • Luis Santos, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about financing needs. Durso and Weaver confirmed a "runway into 2028" but plan to "make further progress as we progress to initiate the trial."

  • William Wood, B. Riley Securities: Queried about cardiovascular endpoints. Arbet-Engels responded, "We believe there is a real potential here to see some cardiovascular benefits. We will clearly look at this in our Phase III MASH trial."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on regulatory pathways, competitive positioning, and financial runway, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, pressing for details on trial design, timelines, and differentiation.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, citing "favorable tolerability," "strong therapeutic potential," and "momentum." During Q&A, management remained measured and occasionally reiterated previous points, particularly regarding regulatory strategy and financial preparedness.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident but shifted to emphasize execution and readiness for late-stage clinical development, while analysts increased focus on specifics of trial timing and differentiation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a new CEO, Jerome Durso, who emphasized strategic team expansion and financial strengthening, compared to the previous quarter's focus on trial milestones and executive appointments.

  • Guidance language evolved to confirm a global Phase III MASH trial with 1,800 patients and specific dosing strategies, whereas previously management emphasized pre-Phase III preparations and regulatory meetings.

  • Analysts in the current quarter concentrated on the details of Phase III design, NIT endpoints, and financial runway, reflecting growing interest in operational execution versus prior focus on trial readouts and regulatory flexibility.

  • Financially, R&D and G&A expenses increased due to executive transitions and trial preparations, with net loss rising versus the prior quarter.

  • Management confidence in pemvidutide's differentiation and market potential was more prominently articulated in the current quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the need to secure additional capital to support clinical programs and noted, "We'll continue to be strategic and opportunistic in our approach to securing access to the forecasted capital needed to fund the Phase III."

  • Regulatory uncertainty remains regarding the use of NITs as registrational endpoints, with management noting, "the agency at that point said it was premature to consider that."

  • Analysts raised questions on competitive dynamics, enrollment timelines, and muscle mass preservation claims.

Final Takeaway

Altimmune management highlighted strong progress toward late-stage development with pemvidutide, underscored by FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation, robust Phase II data, and a clear plan for a global Phase III MASH trial enrolling 1,800 patients. With a strengthened balance sheet and operating cash runway projected into 2028, the company is positioned to advance its pipeline, address unmet needs in liver disease, and differentiate pemvidutide through efficacy, tolerability, and lean muscle preservation as it moves toward pivotal data and potential regulatory approval.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: El Pollo Loco makes menu innovation a big priority for 2026
data.description:
El Pollo Loco (LOCO) plans to make culinary innovation a major focus in 2026. The restaurant chain announced that it is testing five new menu innovations across approximately 60 restaurants, including locations in California and Utah. The market tests will run through late April and will reflect the brand's commitment to expanding flavor exploration and enhancing guest variety.

The items currently in‑market or entering the test phase this month include a Honey Chipotle BBQ fire-grilled chicken, Loco Tenders Crispy 100% all-white-meat chicken tenders, loaded quesadillas, a crispy grilled chicken sandwich, and horchata coffee drinks.

Looking ahead, El Pollo Loco (LOCO) has also committed to eight additional new menu item tests in 2026. Those details will be announced later in the year.

The company is scheduled to release fourth-quarter earnings on March 12.

data.title: Energy ETF still a Quant Buy amid oil price surge as war enters day six
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating keeps the Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) at a solid Buy (4.35 score) despite crude's blistering 20% rally, signaling it's not too late to load up on XLE.

Crude oil prices extended their war-fueled rally on Thursday, up nearly 20% overall, as the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict hit day six, with Strait of Hormuz shipping nearly halted and trapping about 20% of global daily oil flows amid tanker attacks and Iranian drone strikes on Gulf infrastructure. 

The ETF's robust score on valuation, profitability, growth, and momentum holds firm despite the run-up, backed by strong holdings like ExxonMobil and Chevron's dividends.

Amid the rally, it is also notable that not a single Quant-rated energy or commodity-energy ETF has dipped into oversold territory with an RSI below 30, signaling sustained bullish momentum rather than exhaustion.

XLE's appeal lies in its diversification across majors and midstream, offering a hedge against prolonged volatility. With global LNG expansion and domestic energy security themes intact, energy remains a sector rotation favorite.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Roche succeeds in mid-stage trial for obesity drug developed with Zealand
data.description:
Roche (RHHBY) (RHHBF) unit Genentech announced on Thursday that its experimental obesity therapy, petrelintide, developed with Zealand Pharma (ZLDPF), succeeded in a mid-stage trial, causing up to 11% weight loss over 42 weeks on average.

Citing topline data from its Phase 2 ZUPREME-1 study, the company stated that the amylin analog hit the trial’s primary endpoint, causing weight loss with a statistically significant and clinically meaningful effect over 28 weeks across all five dosing arms.

Genentech added that patients on ZUPREME-1 maintained weight loss effects in response to the once-weekly injectable, reporting an average weight loss of up to 10.7% over 42 weeks, compared to 1.7% on placebo.

As for tolerability, the company said that gastrointestinal-related adverse events were the most frequent AEs, and rates of diarrhea and constipation stood in the single-digit range, consistent between on-drug and off-drug arms.

Overall, 8.4% of patients who received petrelintide discontinued the study compared to 13.6% of those on placebo.

The company expects that final results from ZUPREME-1, which it plans to present at a future medical event, will help it design Phase 3 trials for the candidate.

data.title: Equity markets are taking their primary cues from the oil market – Citi’s Pettit
data.description:
Equity markets (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) are currently taking their primary cues from the volatile oil market (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM), according to Citi U.S. equity strategist Drew Pettit.

“Right now, you’re still seeing oil vol spike higher, and the VIX (VIX) is going to chase that higher,” Pettit said in an interview with CNBC. “That leads to these sell-offs.”

The rising crude prices (CL1:COM) are beginning to undermine the strong performance seen in cyclical stocks earlier this year. While macro data and earnings have been positive, Pettit noted that forward-looking inflation differentials are becoming a negative factor.

“We’re denting the cyclical narrative with oil prices,” he explained. “That’s what’s really been driving the market for most of this year.”

On the inflation front, Pettit emphasized that the Federal Reserve may look past energy-driven CPI spikes since “the Fed doesn’t really live on CPI” but rather focuses on core PCE or trimmed mean PCE metrics.

However, he warned that consumer staples are failing to provide traditional defensive protection against rising energy costs.

“Those defensive trades that people wanted to pile in, I would say the bad side of halo to us, they’re not providing defense when oil’s up,” Pettit said.

The strategist favors industrials (XLI), (VIS), (FXR), (IYJ) and infrastructure (MLPX), (DTCR), (IFRA) over airlines (JETS) and consumer-heavy cyclicals, pointing to their ability to pass costs on.

“It’s the areas where you can’t pass along higher input costs where we get concerned about,” he noted, adding that the margin story for infrastructure names remains positive.

Pettit contrasted the business side of the economy, which has free cash flow and immediate CapEx expensing benefits, with a consumer base that is not in the “healthiest spot.”

Consumer companies are not passing along tariffs aggressively, and Pettit questioned whether they would be able to pass along oil costs either.

“Don’t be chasing this defensive low beta rally,” he warned. “It’s not going to provide you defense because it’s been bid up too much.”

The U.S. remains more energy-independent and defensive compared to European markets, which are already potentially pricing in rate hikes in response to rising energy prices.

Pettit described the U.S. market as currently “range bound” rather than experiencing sharp sell-offs, positioning it as a “low beta trade relative to markets like the KOSPI” during this period of energy volatility.

data.title: Wall Street sinks as oil prices rise after renewed Iran war escalations
data.description:
Wall Street's major indexes were drastically lower on Thursday as investors weighed escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and rising oil prices following military confrontations between the U.S. and Iran.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1.2%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1.1%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -2.1%.

Oil prices (CL1:COM) rose 8.5% to top $80 after the IRGC said it would intensify and expand strikes in the coming days, while the U.S. confirmed it had sunk an Iranian warship in the Indian Ocean near Sri Lanka. 

There is also uncertainty on when shipping will resume through the Strait of Hormuz, with U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth offering little clarity on the conflict’s duration, saying “it could be six, it could be eight, it could be three” weeks.

Over in the bond market, U.S. Treasury yields moved higher across the curve amid geopolitical uncertainty. The benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 3 basis points to 4.13%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) gained 4 basis points to 3.60%.

On the U.S. economy, U.S.-based employers announced 55% fewer job cuts in February, down 72% from a year ago, according to the Challenger, Gray & Christmas report. Also, the U.S. Department of Labor reported that initial jobless claims held at 213K for the week ending on Feb. 28.

“The latest Challenger Report for the U.S. is a relief after the shock of its previous monthly release. Following January's layoffs, which surged to their highest levels since 2009, February’s data is significantly less alarming. At 48,307, layoffs were less than half the previous level, while hiring activity picked up,” said Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz.

In addition, nonfarm productivity rose by 2.8% QoQ in Q4 2025, vs. 5.2% (revised from +4.9%) in Q3, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics. Lastly, U.S. import prices rose 0.2% MoM in January, lower than consensus and stalling from the prior month's reading.

In other political news, Ecuador and the U.S. carried out a joint military operation targeting powerful criminal gangs, marking a new phase in Washington’s expanding campaign against drug trafficking groups in Latin America.

Lastly, the U.S. Senate voted to block a Democratic-led resolution that would have restricted President Donald Trump's authority to conduct further military strikes on Iran without congressional approval.
